episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Wild Spaces & Outdoor Life
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Wild Spaces & Outdoor Life.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [CFJC Today Kamloops] CFJC Midday – ‘The Pit’ exhibition transforms the Cube at the Kamloops Art Gallery and [New Atlas] Skyscraper-style tiny house sleeps two in a compact footprint, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: Why do backcountry safety incidents continue to rise in BC despite improved technology and data infrastructure, and what structural changes could actually reduce them? Topics include: The moral hazard of safety technology expanding access without building judgment, Information integration failures: why data exists in portals but not at trailhead decision points, Governance relationships required between commercial platforms and Indigenous nations managing Aboriginal title lands, Tiered access protocols and trip registration as upstream accountability mechanisms, Comparative models: Avalanche Canada forecasting vs. Haida-Parks Canada stewardship vs. potential Chilcotin solutions.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

COMMUNITY SPOTLIGHT: Mt. Timothy Ski Society

discussion.central_question: Why do backcountry safety incidents continue to rise in BC despite improved technology and data infrastructure, and what structural changes could actually reduce them?
discussion.riley_position: Better-designed digital tools can create entry points into stewardship relationships with Indigenous nations, and proven models like Haida-Parks Canada partnerships in Gwaii Haanas demonstrate that governance interfaces between platforms, territories, and users can work.
riley_key_evidence: Avalanche Canada's forecasting system kept fatalities flat despite increased backcountry participation by combining capability with judgment frameworks
Haida Nation stewardship model in Gwaii Haanas successfully created a formal interface between Parks Canada, the Haida Heritage Centre, and backcountry visitors
Trip-planning tools could surface prompts connecting users to Tŝilhqot'in stewardship offices to create invitations for knowledge-holder conversations
discussion.casey_position: Technology creates moral hazard by expanding access without building judgment; the real gap is that data exists in portals but not at decision points, and solving it requires governance agreements and institutional resourcing that haven't been built.
casey_key_evidence: Capability and judgment are separate—communicator devices get people rescued but don't make them competent in backcountry navigation
BC Parks reservation system created a pre-trip engagement window that could attach conditions briefings, showing a model for meaningful pre-access contact
Indigenous stewardship information requires selective disclosure—sensitive cultural sites and wildlife data shouldn't be visible in commercial mapping apps used by millions
discussion.resolution: They converged on the solution that the path forward requires a governance relationship between platforms, Indigenous nations, and users rather than another technology; both agreed that institutional resourcing—not just project funding—must support the nations holding stewardship authority, and that the relationship itself (not the app) is the critical component.
topics_covered: The moral hazard of safety technology expanding access without building judgment
Information integration failures: why data exists in portals but not at trailhead decision points
Governance relationships required between commercial platforms and Indigenous nations managing Aboriginal title lands
Tiered access protocols and trip registration as upstream accountability mechanisms
Comparative models: Avalanche Canada forecasting vs. Haida-Parks Canada stewardship vs. potential Chilcotin solutions
